14	# Lean Scaffolder Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to bridge the gap between the plan agent's strategy and the prover's execution. You inject Lean scaffolding and implementation hints into `.lean` files.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Read Context:**
21	   - Read the planner's directive, `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, and the relevant blueprint chapter to understand the math and the implementation plan.
22	2. **Scaffold Lean Declarations:**
23	   - Create the exact Lean signatures requested by the planner.
24	   - Leave the bodies as `sorry`. Do NOT attempt to prove the theorems.
25	3. **Inject Strategy Comments:**
26	   - Place the planner's implementation ideas directly into the `.lean` file.
27	   - Use comments like `/- Blueprint note: ... -/` or `/- Planner strategy: ... -/` right above the target declaration.
28	   - Translate the high-level strategy into actionable advice for the prover (e.g., "split into these two cases", "use the verified mathlib lemma X").
29	4. **Verify Lean Names:**
30	   - You MUST NOT invent or hallucinate Lean names.
31	   - If the planner suggests a name, or if you are translating a mathematical concept, use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to verify the existence of the Lean function/lemma.
32	   - If a name is unknown, tag it clearly or leave it out.
33	5. **Keep the 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence:**
34	   - Every declaration you scaffold should correspond to a blueprint entry (the directive normally names the chapter and labels — the planner creates the entries before or alongside your dispatch). Lean never exists without tex.
35	   - You cannot write `.tex` (outside your write-domain): when the directive asks you to scaffold a declaration with NO corresponding blueprint entry, scaffold it anyway but list it prominently in your report under "Uncovered declarations" — declaration name, file, and its dependencies — so the planner adds the entry the same iteration.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
138	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
139	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
140	
141	### Subagents
142	
143	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
144	
145	### Review Agent
146	
147	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
148	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
149	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
150	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
151	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
152	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
153	
154	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
155	
156	These run automatically between phases:
157	
158	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
159	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
160	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

71	/-- The scheme-level sheafification functor, sending a presheaf of modules over a
72	scheme `X` to its associated sheaf of modules `X.Modules`.  It is the
73	`PresheafOfModules.sheafification` functor for the identity morphism of the
74	underlying presheaf of rings (which is locally bijective).  Non-private because it
75	appears in the statement of `isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
76	noncomputable def sheafification : X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules :=
77	  PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
78	
79	/-- The category `X.PresheafOfModules` of presheaves of modules over a scheme,
80	presented in the exact form `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)`
81	for which Mathlib equips it with a symmetric monoidal structure.  This is
82	*definitionally* `X.PresheafOfModules` (since
83	`X.ringCatSheaf.obj = X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`), so a term of
84	either type is accepted for the other. -/
85	private abbrev MonoidalPresheaf (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ :=
86	  _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
87	
88	/-- The tensor product of two sheaves of modules over a scheme, defined as the
89	sheafification of the objectwise tensor product presheaf
90	(Mathlib `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  See [Stacks, Tag 01CA]
91	(`def:sheafTensorObj`). -/
92	noncomputable def tensorObj (F G : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
93	  sheafification.obj
94	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
95	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
96	
97	/-- The structure sheaf as a sheaf of modules over itself: the unit object of the
98	tensor product, i.e. the zeroth tensor power `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X`
99	(`lem:moduleUnit_mathlib`). -/
100	private noncomputable abbrev unitModule (X : Scheme.{u}) : X.Modules :=
101	  SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf
102	
103	/-- The `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules over a scheme, defined
104	by recursion: `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X` (the unit module) and
105	`L^{⊗(m+1)} = L^{⊗m} ⊗ L`.  See [Stacks, Tag 01CU] (`def:sheafTensorPow`). -/
106	noncomputable def tensorPow (L : X.Modules) : ℕ → X.Modules
107	  | 0 => unitModule X
108	  | (m + 1) => tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L
109	
110	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_zero (L : X.Modules) : tensorPow L 0 = unitModule X := rfl
111	
112	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_succ (L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) :
113	    tensorPow L (m + 1) = tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L := rfl
114	
115	/-- The `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules `F` by the `m`-th
116	tensor power of a line bundle `L` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`).  This is the
117	degree-`m` carrier of the section graded module. -/
118	noncomputable def moduleTensorPow (F L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) : X.Modules :=
119	  tensorObj F (tensorPow L m)
120	
121	@[simp] private lemma moduleTensorPow_zero (F L : X.Modules) :
122	    moduleTensorPow F L 0 = tensorObj F (unitModule X) := rfl

124	/-! ### Unitor and braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product
125	
126	These are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal structure on `X.Modules`
127	that descend through sheafification from `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
128	using only *functoriality* of `sheafification` (and, for the unitors, the
129	reflective counit iso) — no strong-monoidality of `sheafification` is needed, so
130	they are axiom-clean.  They are the launching pad for `tensorPowAdd`. -/
131	
132	/-- The counit isomorphism of the module sheafification adjunction: sheafifying
133	the underlying presheaf of a sheaf of modules returns the sheaf itself.  This is
134	an isomorphism because the counit of `sheafification ⊣ toPresheafOfModules` is
135	invertible (the right adjoint `SheafOfModules.forget` is fully faithful).  It is
136	the launching pad for the left-unitor base case of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
137	private noncomputable def sheafificationCounitIso (G : X.Modules) :
138	    sheafification.obj ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G) ≅ G :=
139	  (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction
140	    (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).counit).app G
141	
142	/-- The left-unitor isomorphism `unitModule X ⊗ G ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
143	product: the presheaf left unitor `λ_` descended through sheafification, composed
144	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  This is the base case (`m = 0`) of
145	`tensorPowAdd`.  Axiom-clean. -/
146	private noncomputable def tensorObjUnitIso (G : X.Modules) :
147	    tensorObj (unitModule X) G ≅ G :=
148	  sheafification.mapIso
149	      (MonoidalCategory.leftUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
150	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
151	    sheafificationCounitIso G
152	
153	/-- The right-unitor isomorphism `G ⊗ unitModule X ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
154	product: the presheaf right unitor `ρ_` descended through sheafification, composed
155	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  Axiom-clean (no monoidal structure
156	on `X.Modules` is required). -/
157	private noncomputable def tensorObjRightUnitor (G : X.Modules) :
158	    tensorObj G (unitModule X) ≅ G :=
159	  sheafification.mapIso
160	      (MonoidalCategory.rightUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
161	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
162	    sheafificationCounitIso G
163	
164	/-- The braiding isomorphism `F ⊗ G ≅ G ⊗ F` of the sheaf tensor product,
165	descended through sheafification from the symmetric braiding on
166	`X.PresheafOfModules` (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  Axiom-clean: the
167	braiding is pure sheafification-functoriality of the presheaf-level braiding, so
168	no monoidal structure on `X.Modules` is required.  This is the symmetry used in
169	the inductive step of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
170	private noncomputable def tensorBraiding (F G : X.Modules) :
171	    tensorObj F G ≅ tensorObj G F :=
172	  sheafification.mapIso
173	    (BraidedCategory.braiding (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
174	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
175	
176	/-! ### Lax-monoidal global sections: the section multiplication
177	
178	The global-sections functor `Γ(X, -)` is only *lax* monoidal: a pair of global
179	sections does not commute with sheafification, so the multiplication is a map,
180	not an isomorphism.  It is nonetheless `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)`-linear and is the data the
181	section graded ring is built from. -/
182	
183	/-- The **section multiplication** (`def:sectionMul`), the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear map
184	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G) → Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.
185	
186	Its domain `(F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)(X)` is, by the objectwise formula of
187	`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-module
188	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G)` of elementary tensors of global sections; a pair
189	`(σ, τ)` is sent to `σ ⊗ τ`.  Postcomposing with the global-sections component of
190	the sheafification unit `η : P → P^#` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) lands in
191	`Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.  As a morphism in `ModuleCat (Γ(X,𝒪_X))` it is automatically
192	`Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear; this records that linearity.  Axiom-clean: it is pure
193	sheafification-unit naturality, requiring no monoidal structure on `X.Modules`. -/
194	noncomputable def sectionsMul (F G : X.Modules) :
195	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
196	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)).obj (Opposite.op ⊤) ⟶
197	      (tensorObj F G).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤) :=
198	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app
199	      (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
200	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))).app (Opposite.op ⊤)

202	/-! ### The strong-monoidality comparison `isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`
203	
204	Following `analogies/snap-route.md` (Analogue 1) and the blueprint proof of
205	`lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`: module sheafification is the
206	localization functor at the class `W := J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)` of
207	morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying abelian-presheaf morphism is a
208	local isomorphism (a `J.W` for the opens topology `J` on `X`). -/
209	
210	/-- The Grothendieck topology on the opens of the scheme `X`. -/
211	private abbrev opensTopology (X : Scheme.{u}) : GrothendieckTopology (TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :=
212	  Opens.grothendieckTopology (X : TopCat)
213	
214	open MorphismProperty in
215	/-- **Localization criterion for module sheafification.**  The scheme-level module
216	sheafification `sheafification.map f` is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules iff the
217	underlying abelian-presheaf morphism `(PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map f` lies in
218	the weak-equivalence class `J.W` of the opens topology (i.e. is a local isomorphism of
219	abelian-group presheaves).  This is the reduction step of
220	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`: it turns the strong-monoidality comparison
221	into a purely abelian local-isomorphism statement.  Project-local: it specialises
222	`_root_.PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms` to the
223	identity morphism of `X.ringCatSheaf.obj`. -/
224	lemma isIso_sheafification_map_iff {P Q : X.PresheafOfModules} (f : P ⟶ Q) :
225	    IsIso (sheafification.map f) ↔
226	      (opensTopology X).W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).map f) := by
227	  have e := _root_.PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms
228	      (J := opensTopology X) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
229	  constructor
230	  · intro h
231	    have h' : ((MorphismProperty.isomorphisms (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)).inverseImage
232	        (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj))) f := h
233	    rw [← e] at h'
234	    exact h'
235	  · intro h
236	    have h' : (((opensTopology X).W).inverseImage
237	        (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj)) f := h
238	    rw [e] at h'
239	    exact h'
240	
241	/-- **The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism.**  The underlying
242	abelian-presheaf morphism of the module sheafification unit `η_P : P ⟶ P^#` is
243	*definitionally* the abelian sheafification unit `toSheafify J P.presheaf`
244	(`PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app`), which lies
245	in the weak-equivalence class `J.W` of the opens topology by
246	`GrothendieckTopology.W_toSheafify`.  Project-local: this is the `η_P ∈ J.W`
247	ingredient of the abelian-`J.W`-monoidality transfer underlying
248	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
249	lemma localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit (P : X.PresheafOfModules) :
250	    (opensTopology X).W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).map
251	      ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app P)) := by
252	  rw [PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app]
253	  exact (opensTopology X).W_toSheafify _
254	
255	/-- **Sheafification inverts the localization unit.**  `sheafification.map η_P` is an
256	isomorphism of sheaves of modules (the reflective-localization unit becomes invertible
257	after sheafifying).  Obtained by feeding `localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit` through the
258	localization criterion `isIso_sheafification_map_iff`.  Project-local: the `m = 0`
259	launching pad and the un-whiskered special case of
260	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
261	lemma isIso_sheafification_map_unit (P : X.PresheafOfModules) :
262	    IsIso (sheafification.map
263	      ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app P)) :=
264	  (isIso_sheafification_map_iff _).mpr (localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit P)

266	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — relative tensor product as a coequalizer
267	
268	This section builds the **objectwise** content of `lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer`
269	(`relativeTensorCoequalizerIso`): over a commutative ring `S` and `S`-modules `M, N`,
270	the relative tensor product `M ⊗[S] N` is the coequalizer, *in the category of abelian
271	groups*, of the two `S`-action maps
272	
273	  `M ⊗[ℤ] S ⊗[ℤ] N  ⇉  M ⊗[ℤ] N`,    `m ⊗ s ⊗ n ↦ (s • m) ⊗ n`  /  `m ⊗ (s • n)`.
274	
275	This is the Mathlib-absent brick on which the strong-monoidality comparison
276	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit` rests: the underlying abelian presheaf of the
277	presheaf-level relative tensor `P ⊗_p Q` is, objectwise, exactly this coequalizer.  The
278	universal property is the abelian-group universal property of the relative tensor product,
279	packaged by `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`.  Everything here is axiom-clean.
280	
281	The promotion of this objectwise colimit to the presheaf category `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrp`
282	(where colimits are computed objectwise) and the identification of the whiskered unit
283	`η_P ▷ Q` with the induced map of coequalizers are the next steps; see the handoff note. -/
284	
285	namespace RelativeTensorCoequalizer
286	
287	open TensorProduct
288	
289	variable (S : Type u) [CommRing S] (M N : Type u)
290	  [AddCommGroup M] [Module S M] [AddCommGroup N] [Module S N]
291	
292	/-- The `S`-action map `S ⊗[ℤ] N → N`, `s ⊗ n ↦ s • n`, as a `ℤ`-linear map. -/
293	noncomputable def actN : (S ⊗[ℤ] N) →ₗ[ℤ] N :=
294	  TensorProduct.lift (LinearMap.mk₂ ℤ (fun s n => s • n)
295	    (fun s1 s2 n => add_smul s1 s2 n) (fun c s n => smul_assoc c s n)
296	    (fun s n1 n2 => smul_add s n1 n2) (fun c s n => smul_comm s c n))
297	
298	/-- The `S`-action map `M ⊗[ℤ] S → M`, `m ⊗ s ↦ s • m`, as a `ℤ`-linear map. -/
299	noncomputable def actM : (M ⊗[ℤ] S) →ₗ[ℤ] M :=
300	  TensorProduct.lift (LinearMap.mk₂ ℤ (fun m s => s • m)
301	    (fun m1 m2 s => smul_add s m1 m2) (fun c m s => smul_comm s c m)
302	    (fun m s1 s2 => add_smul s1 s2 m) (fun c m s => smul_assoc c s m))
303	
304	/-- Right action map `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) → M ⊗[ℤ] N`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ m ⊗ (s • n)`. -/
305	noncomputable def actRmap : (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
306	  TensorProduct.map LinearMap.id (actN S N)
307	
308	/-- Left action map `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) → M ⊗[ℤ] N`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ (s • m) ⊗ n`. -/
309	noncomputable def actLmap : (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
310	  (TensorProduct.map (actM S M) LinearMap.id).comp
311	    (TensorProduct.assoc ℤ M S N).symm.toLinearMap
312	
313	omit [Module S M] in
314	@[simp] lemma actRmap_tmul (m : M) (s : S) (n : N) :
315	    actRmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)) = m ⊗ₜ (s • n) := rfl
316	
317	omit [Module S N] in
318	@[simp] lemma actLmap_tmul (m : M) (s : S) (n : N) :
319	    actLmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)) = (s • m) ⊗ₜ n := rfl
320	
321	/-- The canonical projection `M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N`, `m ⊗ n ↦ m ⊗ n`, as a `ℤ`-linear
322	map.  It is the cofork map exhibiting `M ⊗[S] N` as the coequalizer. -/
323	noncomputable def projL : (M ⊗[ℤ] N) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[S] N) :=
324	  (TensorProduct.liftAddHom
325	    { toFun := fun m =>
326	        (LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom (((TensorProduct.mk S M N) m).restrictScalars ℤ))
327	      map_zero' := by ext n; simp
328	      map_add' := fun m1 m2 => by ext n; simp }
329	    (fun r m n => by simp)).toIntLinearMap
330	
331	@[simp] lemma projL_tmul (m : M) (n : N) : projL S M N (m ⊗ₜ n) = m ⊗ₜ[S] n := rfl
332	
333	/-- The projection `M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N` is surjective (it is the canonical
334	quotient map onto the relative tensor). -/
335	lemma projL_surjective : Function.Surjective (projL S M N) := by
336	  intro y
337	  induction y using TensorProduct.induction_on with
338	  | zero => exact ⟨0, map_zero _⟩
339	  | tmul m n => exact ⟨m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n, projL_tmul S M N m n⟩
340	  | add a b ha hb =>
341	    obtain ⟨pa, rfl⟩ := ha; obtain ⟨pb, rfl⟩ := hb; exact ⟨pa + pb, map_add _ _ _⟩
342	
343	/-- The two action maps become equal after the projection: this is the cofork
344	coequalizing condition, established at the level of `ℤ`-linear maps. -/
345	lemma projL_comp_act :
346	    (projL S M N).comp (actLmap S M N) = (projL S M N).comp (actRmap S M N) := by
347	  apply TensorProduct.ext'; intro m x
348	  induction x with
349	  | zero => rw [tmul_zero, map_zero, map_zero]
350	  | tmul s n =>
351	    change projL S M N (actLmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)))
352	      = projL S M N (actRmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)))
353	    rw [actLmap_tmul, actRmap_tmul, projL_tmul, projL_tmul, ← TensorProduct.smul_tmul',
354	      TensorProduct.tmul_smul]
355	  | add a b ha hb => rw [tmul_add, map_add, map_add, ha, hb]
356	
357	/-- Left action map as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
358	noncomputable def aL :
359	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
360	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (actLmap S M N).toAddMonoidHom
361	/-- Right action map as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
362	noncomputable def aR :
363	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
364	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (actRmap S M N).toAddMonoidHom
365	/-- The projection as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
366	noncomputable def piMor :
367	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[S] N) :=
368	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (projL S M N).toAddMonoidHom
369	
370	@[simp] lemma piMor_apply (x) : (ConcreteCategory.hom (piMor S M N)) x = projL S M N x := rfl
371	
372	instance piMor_epi : Epi (piMor S M N) :=
373	  ConcreteCategory.epi_of_surjective (piMor S M N) (projL_surjective S M N)
374	
375	/-- The projection coequalizes the two action maps (as morphisms of abelian groups). -/
376	lemma coeq_condition : aL S M N ≫ piMor S M N = aR S M N ≫ piMor S M N := by
377	  ext x; exact LinearMap.congr_fun (projL_comp_act S M N) x
378	
379	/-- The cofork `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) ⇉ M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N` of abelian groups. -/
380	noncomputable def cofork : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N) :=
381	  Limits.Cofork.ofπ (piMor S M N) (coeq_condition S M N)
382	
383	/-- The descent map out of `M ⊗[S] N` induced by a cofork `s`: a pair of global
384	sections balanced under the `S`-action factors through the relative tensor.  This
385	is the universal property packaged by `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`. -/
386	noncomputable def descHom (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) :
387	    (M ⊗[S] N) →+ s.pt :=
388	  TensorProduct.liftAddHom
389	    { toFun := fun m =>
390	        { toFun := fun n => (ConcreteCategory.hom s.π) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n)
391	          map_zero' := by rw [tmul_zero, map_zero]
392	          map_add' := fun n1 n2 => by rw [tmul_add, map_add] }
393	      map_zero' := by ext n; simp [zero_tmul]
394	      map_add' := fun m1 m2 => by ext n; simp [add_tmul] }
395	    (fun a m n => by
396	      simp only [AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk]
397	      have key :=
398	        congrArg (fun φ => (ConcreteCategory.hom φ) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] (a ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n))) s.condition
399	      simpa using key)
400	
401	@[simp] lemma descHom_tmul (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) (m : M) (n : N) :
402	    descHom S M N s (m ⊗ₜ[S] n) = (ConcreteCategory.hom s.π) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n) := rfl
403	
404	/-- The descent map as a morphism of abelian groups out of the cofork apex. -/
405	noncomputable def descMor (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) :
406	    (cofork S M N).pt ⟶ s.pt :=
407	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (descHom S M N s)
408	
409	/-- The descent map factors the cofork's projection: `π ≫ descMor s = s.π`. -/
410	lemma descFac (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) :
411	    (cofork S M N).π ≫ descMor S M N s = s.π := by
412	  ext x
413	  induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with
414	  | zero => simp
415	  | tmul m n =>
416	    change descHom S M N s (projL S M N (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n)) = (ConcreteCategory.hom s.π) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n)
417	    rw [projL_tmul, descHom_tmul]
418	  | add a b ha hb => simp only [map_add, ha, hb]
419	
420	/-- **`M ⊗[S] N` is the coequalizer**, in the category of abelian groups, of the two
421	`S`-action maps `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) ⇉ M ⊗[ℤ] N`.  This is the objectwise content of
422	`lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer`; uniqueness uses that the projection `piMor` is an
423	epimorphism.  Axiom-clean. -/
424	noncomputable def isColimitCofork : Limits.IsColimit (cofork S M N) :=
425	  Limits.Cofork.IsColimit.mk _ (descMor S M N) (descFac S M N)
426	    (fun s _ hf => (cancel_epi (piMor S M N)).mp (hf.trans (descFac S M N s).symm))
427	
428	end RelativeTensorCoequalizer

430	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — presheaf promotion of the coequalizer (Step 1)
431	
432	The objectwise coequalizer `RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork` exhibits, for a fixed
433	open `U`, the relative tensor `Γ(U,P) ⊗_{R(U)} Γ(U,Q)` as a coequalizer of the two
434	`R(U)`-action maps on `Γ(U,P) ⊗_ℤ R(U) ⊗_ℤ Γ(U,Q) ⇉ Γ(U,P) ⊗_ℤ Γ(U,Q)`.  To promote this
435	to the functor category `(Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab` (where colimits are computed objectwise, via
436	`CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits`) one first needs the two **domain
437	presheaves of the cofork as honest functors**, whose restriction maps are the `ℤ`-tensors of
438	the underlying restriction maps.  This section builds the first of those two functors
439	(`relTensorDomainPresheaf`, the `Γ(-,P) ⊗_ℤ Γ(-,Q)` presheaf); it is the concrete Step-1 brick
440	of `lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer` (`relativeTensorCoequalizerIso`).
441	
442	See the handoff note at the end of the file for the verified recipe for the remaining pieces
443	(triple-tensor presheaf, the natural action/projection transformations, the colimit lift, and
444	the apex identification) and the heartbeat/coercion friction points that must be budgeted. -/
445	
446	open scoped TensorProduct
447	
448	/-- Restriction map for a presheaf of modules with syntactic `↥(P.obj U)` carriers.
449	The underlying function is `(P.presheaf.map f).hom`; the type annotation forces the
450	domain/codomain to print as `↥(P.obj U)` / `↥(P.obj V)` (not `↥((P.presheaf).obj U)`,
451	which are rfl-defeq but syntactically distinct).  The syntactic agreement is the
452	load-bearing ingredient for `TensorProduct.map_tmul` unification in
453	`relTensorActL.naturality` / `relTensorActR.naturality`. -/
454	private noncomputable def objRestrict (P : X.PresheafOfModules)
455	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ} (f : U ⟶ V) :
456	    ↥(P.obj U) →ₗ[ℤ] ↥(P.obj V) :=
457	  (show ↥(P.obj U) →+ ↥(P.obj V) from
458	    { toFun := (P.presheaf.map f).hom
459	      map_zero' := map_zero (P.presheaf.map f).hom
460	      map_add' := map_add (P.presheaf.map f).hom }).toIntLinearMap
461	
462	@[simp] private lemma objRestrict_apply (P : X.PresheafOfModules)
463	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ} (f : U ⟶ V) (x : ↥(P.obj U)) :
464	    objRestrict P f x = (P.presheaf.map f).hom x := rfl
465	
466	/-- Identity law for the syntactic-carrier restriction: `objRestrict P (𝟙 U) = id`. -/
467	private lemma objRestrict_id (P : X.PresheafOfModules) (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ) :
468	    objRestrict P (𝟙 U) = LinearMap.id := by
469	  ext x
470	  simp only [objRestrict_apply, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, AddCommGrpCat.hom_id,
471	    AddMonoidHom.id_apply, LinearMap.id_coe, id_eq]
472	
473	/-- Composition law for the syntactic-carrier restriction:
474	`objRestrict P (f ≫ g) = (objRestrict P g) ∘ (objRestrict P f)`. -/
475	private lemma objRestrict_comp (P : X.PresheafOfModules)
476	    {U V W : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ} (f : U ⟶ V) (g : V ⟶ W) :
477	    objRestrict P (f ≫ g) = (objRestrict P g).comp (objRestrict P f) := by
478	  ext x
479	  simp only [objRestrict_apply, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_comp, AddCommGrpCat.hom_comp,
480	    AddMonoidHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply, LinearMap.comp_apply]
481	
482	/-- The objectwise `ℤ`-tensor presheaf `U ↦ Γ(U,P) ⊗_ℤ Γ(U,Q)` of two presheaves of modules
483	over a scheme, as a functor into abelian groups, with restriction maps the `ℤ`-tensors of the
484	two underlying restriction maps.  This is the codomain (apex-adjacent) presheaf of the cofork
485	in the presheaf promotion of `RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork`; it is the concrete
486	Step-1 brick of the presheaf-level coequalizer iso `relativeTensorCoequalizerIso`
487	(`lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer`).  Project-local: no objectwise `ℤ`-tensor of
488	abelian-group presheaves is provided by Mathlib (`AddCommGrpCat` carries no monoidal
489	structure in the current pin). -/
490	noncomputable def relTensorDomainPresheaf (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
491	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab where
492	  obj U := AddCommGrpCat.of (P.obj U ⊗[ℤ] Q.obj U)
493	  map {U V} f := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
494	    (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f) (objRestrict Q f)).toAddMonoidHom
495	  map_id U := by
496	    ext x
497	    induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with
498	    | zero => simp
499	    | tmul m n =>
500	      simp only [AddCommGrpCat.hom_ofHom, LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, TensorProduct.map_tmul,
501	        objRestrict_apply, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, AddCommGrpCat.hom_id,
502	        AddMonoidHom.id_apply]
503	    | add a b ha hb => simp only [map_add, ha, hb]
504	  map_comp {U V W} f g := by
505	    ext x
506	    induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with
507	    | zero => simp
508	    | tmul m n =>
509	      simp only [AddCommGrpCat.hom_ofHom, LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, TensorProduct.map_tmul,
510	        objRestrict_apply, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_comp,
511	        AddCommGrpCat.hom_comp, AddMonoidHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply]
512	    | add a b ha hb => simp only [map_add, ha, hb]
513	
514	/-- The objectwise `ℤ`-tensor triple presheaf `U ↦ Γ(U,P) ⊗_ℤ (𝒪_X(U) ⊗_ℤ Γ(U,Q))` of two
515	presheaves of modules over a scheme, as a functor into abelian groups, with restriction maps the
516	`ℤ`-tensors of the underlying restriction maps (the middle factor restricting via the ring
517	restriction map of `𝒪_X`).  This is the **domain** row of the relative-tensor coequalizer
518	presentation (`lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer`); objectwise it is the triple tensor on which
519	the two `R(U)`-action maps `RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap`/`actRmap` act.  Project-local: no
520	objectwise `ℤ`-tensor of abelian-group presheaves is provided by Mathlib. -/
521	noncomputable def relTensorTriplePresheaf (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
522	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab where
523	  obj U := AddCommGrpCat.of (P.obj U ⊗[ℤ] (X.sheaf.obj.obj U ⊗[ℤ] Q.obj U))
524	  map {U V} f := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
525	    (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f)
526	      (TensorProduct.map (X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap
527	        (objRestrict Q f))).toAddMonoidHom
528	  map_id U := by
529	    have hR : (X.sheaf.obj.map (𝟙 U)).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap =
530	        LinearMap.id (R := ℤ) (M := ↥(X.sheaf.obj.obj U)) := by
531	      ext s
532	      simp only [CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, CommRingCat.hom_id, RingHom.toAddMonoidHom_eq_coe,
533	        AddMonoidHom.coe_toIntLinearMap, LinearMap.id_coe, id_eq]
534	      rfl
535	    rw [objRestrict_id P U, objRestrict_id Q U, hR, TensorProduct.map_id, TensorProduct.map_id]
536	    rfl
537	  map_comp {U V W} f g := by
538	    have hR : (X.sheaf.obj.map (f ≫ g)).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap =
539	        ((X.sheaf.obj.map g).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap).comp
540	          ((X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap) := by
541	      ext s
542	      simp only [CategoryTheory.Functor.map_comp, CommRingCat.hom_comp,
543	        RingHom.toAddMonoidHom_eq_coe, AddMonoidHom.coe_toIntLinearMap, LinearMap.coe_comp,
544	        Function.comp_apply]
545	      rfl
546	    rw [objRestrict_comp P f g, objRestrict_comp Q f g, hR, TensorProduct.map_comp,
547	      TensorProduct.map_comp]
548	    rfl
549	
550	/-- The **left-action** natural transformation of the coequalizer rows
551	(`def:relTensorActL`): `relTensorTriplePresheaf P Q ⟶ relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q`, whose
552	component at `U` is the objectwise left-action map
553	`RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap` collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar
554	action of `𝒪_X(U)` on `Γ(U,P)`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ (s • m) ⊗ n`.  Naturality in `U` is the
555	compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps, checked on elementary tensors
556	by `⊗`-induction (the single fact `PresheafOfModules.map_smul`, bridged to the abelian
557	restriction by `objRestrict_apply`). -/
558	noncomputable def relTensorActL (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
559	    relTensorTriplePresheaf P Q ⟶ relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q where
560	  app U := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
561	    (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap (X.sheaf.obj.obj U) (P.obj U) (Q.obj U)).toAddMonoidHom
562	  naturality {U V} f := by
563	    -- The underlying ℤ-linear naturality square, proven by `⊗`-induction.  The single
564	    -- mathematical fact is `PresheafOfModules.map_smul` (semilinearity of the restriction).
565	    have key :
566	        (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap (↥(X.sheaf.obj.obj V)) (↥(P.obj V)) (↥(Q.obj V))).comp
567	            (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f)
568	              (TensorProduct.map (X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap
569	                (objRestrict Q f)))
570	          = (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f) (objRestrict Q f)).comp
571	              (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap (↥(X.sheaf.obj.obj U)) (↥(P.obj U))
572	                (↥(Q.obj U))) := by
573	      apply TensorProduct.ext'
574	      intro m y
575	      induction y using TensorProduct.induction_on with
576	      | zero => simp
577	      | tmul s n =>
578	        change ((X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap s • objRestrict P f m)
579	              ⊗ₜ[ℤ] objRestrict Q f n
580	            = objRestrict P f (s • m) ⊗ₜ[ℤ] objRestrict Q f n
581	        congr 1
582	        rw [objRestrict_apply, objRestrict_apply]
583	        exact (PresheafOfModules.map_smul P f s m).symm
584	      | add a b ha hb => simp only [map_add, ha, hb, TensorProduct.tmul_add]
585	    -- Transport the linear-map square to the categorical naturality square in `Ab`.
586	    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
587	    dsimp only [relTensorTriplePresheaf, relTensorDomainPresheaf]
588	    ext z
589	    have hz := LinearMap.congr_fun key z
590	    simpa only [AddCommGrpCat.hom_comp, AddCommGrpCat.hom_ofHom, AddMonoidHom.comp_apply,
591	      LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, LinearMap.comp_apply] using hz
592	
593	/-- The **right-action** natural transformation of the coequalizer rows
594	(`def:relTensorActR`): `relTensorTriplePresheaf P Q ⟶ relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q`, whose
595	component at `U` is the objectwise right-action map
596	`RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap` collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar
597	action of `𝒪_X(U)` on `Γ(U,Q)`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ m ⊗ (s • n)`.  Naturality is the
598	compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps (`PresheafOfModules.map_smul`
599	on `Q`), checked on elementary tensors by `⊗`-induction. -/
600	noncomputable def relTensorActR (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
601	    relTensorTriplePresheaf P Q ⟶ relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q where
602	  app U := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
603	    (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap (X.sheaf.obj.obj U) (P.obj U) (Q.obj U)).toAddMonoidHom
604	  naturality {U V} f := by
605	    have key :
606	        (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap (↥(X.sheaf.obj.obj V)) (↥(P.obj V)) (↥(Q.obj V))).comp
607	            (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f)
608	              (TensorProduct.map (X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap
609	                (objRestrict Q f)))
610	          = (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f) (objRestrict Q f)).comp
611	              (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap (↥(X.sheaf.obj.obj U)) (↥(P.obj U))
612	                (↥(Q.obj U))) := by
613	      apply TensorProduct.ext'
614	      intro m y
615	      induction y using TensorProduct.induction_on with
616	      | zero => simp
617	      | tmul s n =>
618	        change objRestrict P f m
619	              ⊗ₜ[ℤ] ((X.sheaf.obj.map f).hom.toAddMonoidHom.toIntLinearMap s • objRestrict Q f n)
620	            = objRestrict P f m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] objRestrict Q f (s • n)
621	        congr 1
622	        rw [objRestrict_apply, objRestrict_apply]
623	        exact (PresheafOfModules.map_smul Q f s n).symm
624	      | add a b ha hb => simp only [map_add, ha, hb, TensorProduct.tmul_add]
625	    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
626	    dsimp only [relTensorTriplePresheaf, relTensorDomainPresheaf]
627	    ext z
628	    have hz := LinearMap.congr_fun key z
629	    simpa only [AddCommGrpCat.hom_comp, AddCommGrpCat.hom_ofHom, AddMonoidHom.comp_apply,
630	      LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, LinearMap.comp_apply] using hz
631	
632	/-- The **projection** natural transformation (`relTensorProj`):
633	`relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q ⟶ (toPresheaf).obj (P ⊗_p Q)`, whose component at `U` is the
634	canonical quotient `RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL` from the objectwise `ℤ`-tensor onto the
635	relative tensor `Γ(U,P) ⊗_{𝒪_X(U)} Γ(U,Q)` (the apex of the cofork, identified with the value of
636	the presheaf monoidal tensor by `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj`).  This is the cofork
637	map of the presheaf-level coequalizer presentation `relativeTensorCoequalizerIso`. -/
638	noncomputable def relTensorProj (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
639	    relTensorDomainPresheaf P Q ⟶
640	      (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj
641	        (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X) P Q) where
642	  app U := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
643	    (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL (X.sheaf.obj.obj U) (P.obj U) (Q.obj U)).toAddMonoidHom
644	  naturality {U V} f := by
645	    -- NATURALITY (the square `projL_V ∘ domain.map f = apex.map f ∘ projL_U`).  We prove the
646	    -- underlying `ℤ`-linear square as `key` and transport it to the categorical square in `Ab`.
647	    -- An element-level `⊗`-induction at the `Ab` level is blocked by the `AddCommGrpCat.of` carrier
648	    -- instance mismatch (`map_add` fails to fire on the bundled `Ab`-morphism applied to `a + b`);
649	    -- working with bare `ℤ`-linear maps and `TensorProduct.ext'` sidesteps it entirely.  On an
650	    -- elementary tensor `m ⊗ₜ n` both composites send it to
651	    -- `(objRestrict P f m) ⊗ₜ[R(V)] (objRestrict Q f n)` definitionally: the LHS via
652	    -- `TensorProduct.map`+`projL`, the RHS via `projL`+`tensorObj_map_tmul`
653	    -- (both `⊗ₜ`-on-the-nose).  The `S = X.sheaf.obj.obj V` vs `R.obj V` base-ring discrepancy is a
654	    -- `forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`-identity, so the elementary-tensor case is `rfl` (no instance
655	    -- re-synthesis, since the existing goal instances are reused).
656	    have key :
657	        (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL (↑(X.sheaf.obj.obj V)) (↑(P.obj V)) (↑(Q.obj V))).comp
658	            (TensorProduct.map (objRestrict P f) (objRestrict Q f))
659	          = (AddCommGrpCat.Hom.hom
660	                (((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj
661	                  (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X) P Q)).map
662	                    f)).toIntLinearMap.comp
663	              (RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL (↑(X.sheaf.obj.obj U)) (↑(P.obj U))
664	                (↑(Q.obj U))) := by
665	      apply TensorProduct.ext'
666	      intro m n
667	      rfl
668	    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
669	    ext z
670	    have hz := LinearMap.congr_fun key z
671	    simpa only [AddCommGrpCat.hom_comp, AddCommGrpCat.hom_ofHom, AddMonoidHom.comp_apply,
672	      LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, LinearMap.comp_apply, AddMonoidHom.coe_toIntLinearMap] using hz
673	
674	/-- The cofork condition for the presheaf-level relative-tensor coequalizer: the left- and
675	right-action rows compose equally with the projection, `a_L ≫ π = a_R ≫ π`, as natural
676	transformations of `(Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab`.  Objectwise it is
677	`RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition`. -/
678	lemma relTensorActL_proj_eq (P Q : X.PresheafOfModules) :
679	    relTensorActL P Q ≫ relTensorProj P Q = relTensorActR P Q ≫ relTensorProj P Q := by
680	  ext U : 2
681	  exact RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition (X.sheaf.obj.obj U) (P.obj U) (Q.obj U)
682	
683	/- Planner strategy: 3-step promotion (blueprint `lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer` proof):
684	1. OBJECTWISE — at each `U`, instantiate `RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork` with
685	   `S = O_X(U)`, `M = P(U)`, `N = Q(U)`. (API DONE axiom-clean.)
686	2. PROMOTE — the three objectwise families ARE `relTensorActL`/`relTensorActR`/`relTensorProj`

688	   `CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits` [Mathlib, verify with leansearch].
689	   NOTE (iter-063): leansearch only finds `CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsLimits`

1131	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1132	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1133	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1134	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1135	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1136	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1137	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1138	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to

1140	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1141	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1142	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1143	  power isomorphisms).}
1144	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1145	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1146	  \[
1147	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1148	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1149	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1150	  \]
1151	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1152	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1153	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1154	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1155	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1156	\end{lemma}

1158	\begin{proof}
1159	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1160	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1161	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1162	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1163	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1164	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1165	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1166	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1167	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1168	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1169	
1170	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1171	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1172	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1173	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1174	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1175	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1176	  sheaf tensor products
1177	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1178	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1179	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1180	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1181	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1182	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1183	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1184	  of the tensor product is never used.
1185	
1186	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1187	  \(m = 0\),
1188	  \[
1189	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1190	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1191	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1192	  \]
1193	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1194	  recursive definition
1195	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1196	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1197	  \[
1198	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1199	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1200	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1201	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1202	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1203	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1204	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1205	  \]
1206	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1207	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1208	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1209	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1210	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1211	  \[
1212	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1213	      \mathcal{L}
1214	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1215	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1216	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1217	  \]
1218	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1219	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1220	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1221	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1222	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1223	
1224	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1225	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1226	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1227	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison

1234	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison

1245	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1246	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1247	
1248	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1249	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1250	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1251	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1252	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1253	global sections.
1254	
1255	\begin{definition}\leanok
1256	[Section multiplication]
1257	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1259	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1260	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1261	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1262	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1263	  \[
1264	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1265	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1266	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1267	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1268	  \]
1269	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1270	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1271	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1272	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1273	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1274	  the sheafification unit
1275	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1276	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1277	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1278	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1279	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1280	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1281	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1282	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1283	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1284	\end{definition}
1285	
1286	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1287	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1289	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1290	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1291	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1292	  the two composites
1293	  \[
1294	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1295	    \longrightarrow
1296	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1297	    \cong
1298	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1299	  \]
1300	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1301	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1302	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1303	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1304	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1305	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1306	\end{lemma}
1307	
1308	\begin{proof}
1309	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1310	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1311	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1312	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1313	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1314	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1315	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1316	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1317	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1318	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1319	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1320	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1321	  section multiplications.
1322	\end{proof}

1324	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1325	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1326	
1327	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1328	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1329	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1330	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1331	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1332	
1333	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1334	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1335	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1336	  \mathlibok
1337	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1338	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1339	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1340	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1341	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1342	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1343	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
